Thank you for answering the aforementioned questions. That's quite helpful.

The first compromising approach that comes to mind is to percent encode `=`s in
values if and only if the associated key is empty. Otherwise, leave any `=`s in
the value as-is.

furl stores queries like `?===3===` as `omdict1D([('', '==3===')])`. `key=value`
query params are stored like `omdict1D([('key', 'value')])`. In turn, it's
straightforward for furl to differentiate between queries like `===3===` and
`which=1%3D1`.

I'll ruminate on this more.

furl should adopt the least surprising approach.

It shall be done.

Unfortunately, the docs aren't rendering the docstrings of the class, which indicate that the `server_list` parameter accepts tuples suitable for constructing `ServerSpec` objects, which take a host, port, and password. So pass something like `server_list = [('myserver', 6667, 'mysecret')]`.

Now that's if your server requires a password. If you need to send something like a Nickserv password, that's a different matter. For something like that, see what the [pmxbot project](https://github.com/yougov/pmxbot) does.

Setting reasonable ranges for the parameters and dropping bad augmentations is probably the best one can do with the library right now. Changing this to a parameter like `keep_keypoints_visible` would likely be very difficult to get into the library, because currently images and keypoints are augmented in completely separate steps and are only "synchronized" by the determinism, while such a parameter would require some kind of one-step handling. It would also break the current philosophy that parameters should only be sampled conditionally dependent on the random state and image shape.

I think that the root of the problem is actually earlier. Taking the important parts from `util.py`
(In Python 2)

```python
>>> import tokenize
>>> from StringIO import StringIO
>>> tokenizer = lambda input_string: tokenize.generate_tokens(StringIO(input_string).readline)
```

The result is:
```python
>>> list(tokenizer('umeter'))
[(1, 'umeter', (1, 0), (1, 6), 'umeter'), (0, '', (2, 0), (2, 0), '')]
>>> list(tokenizer(u'µmeter'))
[(52, u'\xb5', (1, 0), (1, 1), u'\xb5meter'), (1, u'meter', (1, 1), (1, 6), u'\xb5meter'), (0, '', (2, 0), (2, 0), '')]
```

The micro prefix in unicode is:
- interpreted as an ERRORTOKEN (code 52)
- splitted from the unit name.

May be we can make a workaround in the parserhelper or write another tokenizer.

I do not think that the `__array_finalize__` is the general solution (but it would be great). 

It is related to the issue that I reported in NumPy. NumPy functions do not like duck typing. You **MUST** derive from ndarrary to make some functions work because `np.asanyarray()` which is called frequently only allows only strict subclasses.

From the beginning, Pint has supported a big chunk of NumPy without requiring you to import anything except numpy. And I mean real support: the one that you do `cos(8 meters)` and you get an Error. But this issue that I mentioned before has prevented us to have full support. In fact, all not working functions which are mentioned [here](http://pint.readthedocs.org/en/0.6/numpy.html#support) are due to this problem.

Quantity does not derive from `ndarray`.  I have tried it in [this branch](https://github.com/hgrecco/pint/tree/_npsubclass) but I am still not sure that is a good idea. I am open to suggestions though.

After looking at [this](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/5570795/how-does-bash-tab-completion-work) and [this](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/9286792/how-is-tab-completion-implemented-for-linux-commands), it looks like thefuck would have to be implemented in slightly different ways on different shells (fish, zsh, bash, etc.). For example, with `bash` all that is needed to be done is for the completion to be registered using `complete` or `compgen`, which would not work for zsh. So shouldn't be hard, just add a little shell checking in the installation script to set up tab completion for all the shells (or just the primary one) installed on the system.

Hi all,

I just understand why my intrinsic modules usage are correctly handled and your not. In my OCD workflows I declare explicitly their are intrinsic module by the `intrinsic` keyword, thus FoBiS has a specific regex to catch these cases, but in your example I think your source is like `use iso_c_binding` and not `use, intrinsic:: iso_c_binding` consequently, due to the absence of keyword `intrinsic` FoBiS assumes that it is a standard module.

I like your feature request to collect the warning on missing dependencies and trying always to make the building, this fixes also the intrinsic module issue, but I am thinking to change their trap: I do not like false positive warning about them... the standard defines their name thus I think it is better to catch them explicitly. Hi @foxx,

I'm assuming that you're referring to the [JTI claim](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.7) that can be used to provide a unique identifier for the JWT in order to prevent replay attacks?

Nothing PyJWT will stop you from using this header if you want to. However, by definition, this header requires you to keep track of previously seen JTIs in order to be useful, its usage is outside the scope of the library and better served by your application.

For instance, your application might use a Redis or Memcache database to set a key value matching the JTI whenever the token is seen. If your application receives the same token again, it can prevent replay by simply looking up the JTI in the database and verifying that it is not present (meaning it has not been seen before). Obviously, something like this is outside the scope of a library to parse and verify JWTs (like PyJWT).

However, nothing stops you from setting the `jti` claim on a token or retrieving the `jti` from a decoded token. I hope that answers your question.

We would also want some "hole-punching" mechanisms: If a manual reply was sent e.g. in a conversation, automated replies should be allowed until a timeout – unless overridden by the application.

To consider: It might make sense to put the aliveness checks into/onto the XML stream, because that’s what’s being tested. Hooking into data_received there is trivial, and that’s what we want to look at anyways. This also prevents huge stanzas on slow links from causing timeouts.

The aliveness check would then just emit events on the softlimit (too much time without server data) and abort the stream on the hardlimit (no data for too long after the softlimit).

The stanza stream would listen to the events of the aliveness check and emit a ``<r/>`` or IQ-ping when the softlimit is reached. It can also configure the limits depending on the measured RTT (cf. #96).

There are a couple of problems:  The big one is that the watch events that the python `inotify` code returns for the (renamed) file still all report the old filename, not the new one, so that events from both files are both identified with the same tag/filename (so it's not possible to tell which is which).

Even if this weren't the case, tracking the filename changes can be tricky or impossible (for example, if the file is actually deleted, it has no new filename, but may still continue to receive writes to it from programs which still have it open).  Likewise, if it's moved/renamed to somewhere outside of the watched directory, I will never receive notification of what it's new filename is (not as likely with my particular application, but still potentially possible, and potentially a problem for other use cases).

The long and the short of it is that the kernel inotify function uses distinct (numerical) handles to refer to watches for a reason, and that reason is because filenames are not a reliable way to track files/watches under a variety of possible (and even, in some cases, likely) scenarios.

FWIW, #19 seems to be similar in some ways but is not the same issue as I'm encountering.  I am not using InotifyTree at all, I'm just trying to watch a few specific files within a single directory, but those files may change (or lose altogether) their filenames during the watching, and other new files (which I also want to watch) may then have exactly the same filename as the original file had, and the current python `inotify` behavior makes that pretty much impossible to distinguish between or account for.

Regarding speed: I tried doubling the SPI clock rate. Things I learned:
- no visible change in "frame rate"
- it is indeed the hardware SPI being used
- the SPI speed setting does affect the clock rate, I tried up to 16000000 and also 15600000 (see below)
- SSD1322 seems to handle this speed even over jumper wires, despite the 100 ns clock cycle time minimum (datasheet v1.2 p. 53) 
- [py-spidev](https://github.com/doceme/py-spidev/blob/master/spidev_module.c#L841) also seems to accept "odd" rates, so [this assert in serial.py#L251](https://github.com/rm-hull/luma.core/blob/master/luma/core/interface/serial.py#L251) may not be neccessary
  * In contrast, [this post](http://www.takaitra.com/posts/492) actually suggests only certain "odd" values are acceptable by RPi. 
  * This is where it gets intricate as the different models' CPUs may differ: 
    * https://www.raspberrypi.org/documentation/hardware/raspberrypi/spi/README.md#hardware
    * https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raspberry_Pi#Specifications
- SPI frame data transfer only every 150-200 ms, matching the approx 5 Hz determined by bounce.py
- the interface is not the bottleneck, as you suggested already

The problem with lxml+PyPy was that PyPy's C api layer was never good enough to run lxml. The official advice used to be that on PyPy you should use their forked lxml-cffi library instead, but just recently (last week) they released a new version of PyPy that fixes the underlying bugs, and now lxml is supported: http://morepypy.blogspot.com.co/2016/02/c-api-support-update.html?m=1

So if you're using the latest version of lxml and PyPy then it should work now (and if it doesn't then probably you should file bugs with PyPy, not lxml).

I also experienced this issue. I noticed SQLAlchemy connection leaks and it turned out it was a GC issue (cyclic references in traceback objects) caused by some APScheduler jobs raising exceptions.

@agronholm For what it's worth: in py3, tracebacks are also emebedded in the exception object. I managed to fix the leak by calling `traceback.clear_frames(exc.__traceback__)` in addition to deleting the traceback object.

```python
@@ -134,4 +137,10 @@ def run_job(job, jobstore_alias, run_times, logger_name):
                                             retval=retval))
             logger.info('Job "%s" executed successfully', job)
 
-    return events
+    try:
+        return events
+    finally:
+        if six.PY3:
+            traceback.clear_frames(exc.__traceback__)
+        del tb
```

If we need a clean `__traceback__` later on (which I don't think because it's unsupported on PY2 anyways), it's still possible to override `__del__` method from `JobExecutionEvent` and delete instances manually in `_run_job_success`. Hope that helps.

There's the same kind of potential leak in `_do_submit_job` callback. Everytime a traceback object is referenced (or an exception in PY3), it should be deleted manually.

@deanmalmgren regarding binary i/o.

I'm not sure if I interpreted [this place](https://github.com/deanmalmgren/textract/blob/master/textract/parsers/utils.py#L44-L46) correctly:

```python
byte_string = self.extract(filename, **kwargs)
unicode_string = self.decode(byte_string)
return self.encode(unicode_string, encoding)
```

But it looks like you are using Unicode sandwich approach, where `Parser.extract` returns bytes and `Parser.process` returns bytes too.

Since `Parser.process` returns bytes, `--output` file have to be opened in binary mode (`wb`) for sure.

But for `self.extract` part I'm not sure, it looks like you expecting bytes (`byte_string = self.extract...`), but `self.decode` accepts anything and returns unicode employing decoding from bytes if needed.

Since information about how to decode binary to unicode is only available for each parser, so it is logical, that `self.extract` should return unicode and each parser should be responsible for returning unicode.

So if you agree, that `self.extract` should return unicode, then I could refactor that place making sure, that all parsers returning unicode. In that case, there is no need for `serf.decode` call in `BaseParser.process`, it should be moved by each parser if input encoding is unknown and `chardet` have to be used.

Here's a possible alternative.

We don't ever copy default values, but we do raise an exception when the default value's top-level type is list, dict or set. To create a value with a default that's an empty list, you have to write something like
```py
    x: list = field(default_factory=list)
```
The error message (as well as the documentation) should guide the user in this direction. The docs should also show that to create a non-empty list you'd have to use a lambda, e.g.
```py
    x: list = field(default_factory=lambda: [0 ,1, 2])
```
I'm not particular about the exact idiom used for `default_factory` -- we could also use
```py
    x: list = field(default=Factory(list, [0, 1, 2])  # Assumes Factory is imported
```
(One thing I don't want is `default=list` -- a default might well be a function.)

If we adopt this proposal, I think we should not do anything about other types of mutable values (@glyph's example of a global db connection should just work) nor about e.g. a tuple containing a list. (Users are still expected to learn that defaults are shared between instances.)

An alternative solution for Glyph's example would be for the db connection class to define a `__copy__` method that just returns `self`, but that feels unsatisfactory because it would require a change to the package implementing db connections. Alternatively the user would have to know to use a factory that just returns the global db connection, e.g.
```py
    connection: DbConnection = field(default_factory=lambda: global_db_connection)
```
All in all I'm not sure I like this better than the shallow-copy-everything proposal, but at least it's easy to explain the first approximation (without the exceptions for list/dict/set) and guides users around the most common mistakes (surely an empty list or dict as a default must be a very common beginner's mistake). I'm also not sure that it is more important to support Glyph's global-db-connection example than it is to have a concise notation for defaults with type list/dict/set -- it's hard to come up with a spec that does the right thing for *both* without using a factory for at least one of them.

I don't worry about the performance, the code generator can omit copy calls for common immutable types and we can hint at that in the docs.

Hi @BogdanL !

Can you show me a code example of how you use this option?

Tracking m2m fields is a bit different than the rest of the fields in `django-dirtyfields`. The original idea of this package is to track a difference between your current instance in memory with the instance that has been previously saved in the database.

As m2m fields are directly saved to the database (overtime you call `.add()` for example), you can't really have an in-memory instance different than the one in the db.

I have tried to explain it in the documentation (http://django-dirtyfields.readthedocs.io/en/develop/#checking-many-to-many-fields), but if you think it's unclear, feel free to propose a better wording. 

And if I missed/misunderstood your current situation, please tell me.

I noticed a similar issue to disimone and I think I've discovered the root cause. In the COXPHFitter class, the baseline hazards are being computed within the fit function. This passes in a version of the data set which has already been normalized. This is an issue because with the compute_baseline_hazard function, the first step is to calculate partial hazards, which normalizes the data again.

I think the solution is just to add an unnormalize step in the fit function, making line 321 something like:

        self.baseline_hazard_ = self._compute_baseline_hazards(unnormalize(df,self._norm_mean,self._norm_std), T, E)

After doing some digging, [`@cached_property`](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/utils/functional.py#L19) caches the property on the instance. The `DatabaseWrapper` class is used to establish a connection to the database, and a new connection is established on each request. Since the value is cached on the _instance_ and not the _class_, this means that the property will be evaluated each time `DatabaseWrapper` is instantiated, which is every time a request is made.

I believe this could be solving by caching the property on the class instead of the instance. @michiya will you merge that change if I open a PR?

```

and then in admin:

```
class SpecificationAdmin(SortableTabularInline):
    model = ProductSpecification
    extra = 1

    def formfield_for_foreignkey(self, db_field, request, **kwargs):
        lang = get_language()

        if db_field.name == "specification":
            kwargs["queryset"] = File.objects.filter(language_code=lang)

        return super(SpecificationAdmin, self).formfield_for_foreignkey(db_field, request, **kwargs)

    def get_queryset(self, request):

        lang = get_language()

        qs = super(SpecificationAdmin, self).get_queryset(request)
        qs = qs.filter(specification__language_code=lang)

        return qs
```

@abastardi 
> Now we have:
> 1. Calculate computed values, fill in default values, and check for missing required fields.
> 2. Run the _before_[insert/update] callbacks.

That's not true, the actual flow is:
1. Enqueue for computation or fill default
2. Check required fields empty from point 1
3. Compute computations enqueued from point 1
4. Run callbacks

I think moving callbacks before point 2 is wrong for two reasons:
- you won't have default values filled in callbacks, and if your callbacks relies on computed or fields that are required, it won't work
- you will have to double check empty values

Also, the example given is pointless since it can be achieved putting a default on the involved fields.
Plus, if you want to make things to fields before computation, why you don't just put the modification code on computation?

My final point: the best option I can thing to have a correct flow for everyone here is to add another callback method, for example `before_validation` and put it between point 1 and 2.

As far as I know, mxnet does not support subpixel upsampling layer yet. Still, you can emulate it using a series of transpose and reshape layers. I am currently using the followings, messy but works anyway.

``` python
def subpixel_upsample(data, ch, c, r, name=None):
    '''
    Transform input data shape of (n, ch*r*c, h, w) to (n, ch, h*r, c*w).

    ch: number of channels after upsample
    r: row scale factor
    c: column scale factor
    '''
    if r == 1 and c == 1:
        return data
    X = mx.sym.reshape(data=data, shape=(-3, 0, 0))  # (n*ch*r*c, h, w)
    X = mx.sym.reshape(data=X, shape=(-4, -1, r * c, 0, 0))  # (n*ch, r*c, h, w)
    X = mx.sym.transpose(data=X, axes=(0, 3, 2, 1))  # (n*ch, w, h, r*c)
    X = mx.sym.reshape(data=X, shape=(0, 0, -1, c))  # (n*ch, w, h*r, c)
    X = mx.sym.transpose(data=X, axes=(0, 2, 1, 3))  # (n*ch, h*r, w, c)
    X = mx.sym.reshape(data=X, name=name, shape=(-4, -1, ch, 0, -3))  # (n, ch, h*r, w*c)
    return X
```

I think I may have spotted the problem, assuming you're still using the same pattern to generate `ZipkinAttrs`. If `ZipkinAttrs` are passed in, but `is_sampled` is set to false, the decorator will generate new attributes for the span, which will override the `parent_span_id` you constructed the the message ID. [Relevant code](https://github.com/Yelp/py_zipkin/blob/v0.1.1/py_zipkin/zipkin.py#L168).

This is intentional - it supports the use case of sampling a component of your system independently of its upstream components. I.e., you might want to produce "stage3:worker" spans at a different rate than "stage2:consumer" spans, so you sample an additional % of "stage3:worker" spans in the cases where the "stage2:consumer" stage isn't sampled.

The solution to this is to get rid of the `sample_rate` kwarg in the "stage3:worker" span, then set the `is_sampled` part of the `ZipkinAttrs` in "stage3:worker" to True in the cases where the corresponding "stage2:consumer" is sampled. Like @kaisen said, you really need a way to propagate this information. Can you add additional information into the `message` payload?

In the absence of the ability to propagate, you can at least test this by sampling everything in the "stage2:consumer" phase and just setting `is_sampled=True` in the "stage3:worker" phase.

I was able to build it with the following PKGBUILD and patch from the archlinux tensorflow PKGBUILD:

```bash
# Maintainer: Jonas Heinrich <onny@project-insanity.org>
# Contributor: Jonas Heinrich <onny@project-insanity.org>

pkgname=deepspeech
pkgver=v0.1.1.r67.gae146d0
pkgrel=1
pkgdesc="A TensorFlow implementation of Baidu's DeepSpeech architecture"
arch=('x86_64')
url="https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech"
license=('MPL2')
makedepends=('bazel' 'python-numpy' 'python-pip' 'python-wheel' 'python-setuptools' 'git')
depends=('python-tensorflow' 'python-scipy' 'sox')
source=("git+https://github.com/mozilla/deepspeech.git"
		"git+https://github.com/mozilla/tensorflow.git#branch=r1.6"
		17508.patch)
sha512sums=('SKIP'
            'SKIP')

pkgver() {
  cd "$pkgname"
  git describe --long --tags | sed 's/\([^-]*-g\)/r\1/;s/-/./g'
}

prepare()
{
	patch -Np1 -i ${srcdir}/17508.patch -d tensorflow
  cd "$srcdir/tensorflow"

  # These environment variables influence the behavior of the configure call below.
  export PYTHON_BIN_PATH=/usr/bin/python
  export USE_DEFAULT_PYTHON_LIB_PATH=1
  export TF_NEED_JEMALLOC=1
  export TF_NEED_GCP=0
  export TF_NEED_HDFS=0
  export TF_NEED_S3=0

It gets worse though, even without an enterprise build system.

Here's a simple experiment that proves how broken this is.
Build something with shiv (project A) in one terminal session and in another build another app that does **not** use shiv (project B) -- at the same time. For example start commands one after the other. The project B build will break because of the custom `~/.pydistutils.cfg` stored during its build from project A build.

This proves that changing *shared* _distutils_ settings in `~/pydistutils.cfg` is not an acceptable solution at all, under any circumstances. If the build is going to change _distutils_ settings at all, it must do that **local** to the project built by _shiv_ **only**.

Thus, as I suggested, let's revert this bug, to get a good release, and than carefully plan a workaround for _homebrew_ Python users that is localized to one project build.

milliseconds or so on my linux box. I'm guessing it will be slower on
windows, but probably not egregiously so.

1. Sure. But is it a big deal? We could say the same thing if a sentence would have been reworded. 2. This is a format adopted by `babel` The reason behind this change is cosmetic. I have built a tool to format YAML files and errors are handled this way: ```js try { data = yaml.load(contents) } catch (e) { const line = e.mark.line + 1 const column = e.mark.column + 1 console.error(`${e.name}: ${e.reason} (${line}:${column})\n`) console.error(codeFrame(contents, line, column)) process.exit(1) } ``` The goal is to be able to switch to compact mode to prevent the code frame from being displayed but keeping the mark concise: * Using `YAMLException#toString()` dumps code 👎 * Using `YAMLException#toString(true)` hides location 👎 Modifying `Mark#toString(true)` to be less verbose and adding it to `YAMLException`'s compact output could be a good tradeoff between debugging info and length.

Hi @rmfitzpatrick. The short answer is that we haven't done anything about better string support since the initial push to support python 3. I believe the reason for this requirement at the moment is that the consumer group string can be used in contexts where a bytestring is required, especially the protocol implementation. Since Kafka itself only accepts ascii strings as consumer group names, we wanted to avoid doing any magical transformations on passed unicode strings to make them fit. I do think this should be updated, though. You can see progress and discussion on more complete unicode support in pykafka in #405.

Looks like you're using [this logic](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/f001ccbf37b55248abcd00732f2f296884821081/pykafka/cluster.py#L94) to have Kafka autocreate a topic with a single partition for you. It actually doesn't [look like](http://kafka.apache.org/documentation.html#topic-config) Kafka currently supports configuring the default number of partitions on autocreated topics. In fact, the way topics are created [in the tests](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/6035a91e5de93c9ffaec9c6b46844d6c4dba74b9/pykafka/test/kafka_instance.py#L93-L96) is to shell out to the Kafka command line tool.

PyKafka currently doesn't have its own way to create topics - in fact, since there's no API for doing so, the only way PyKafka *could* even do it at the moment would be this method of shelling out to the Kafka CLI. I recommend either simply sticking with the Kafka CLI-based method or pull requesting an addition of a Python wrapper for that tool into `cli/kafka_tools.py`.

@arcivanov I am using `FluentHandler` and the `FluentFormatter` (although I subclass it to add an extra tracking ID field to the log).

However, the `extra` parameter is ignored, unless the `FluentFormatter` is instantiated with `exclude_attrs=[]`, which causes it to use the `_format_by_exclusion` method instead of `_format_by_dict` and the extra fields are then added to the dict (along with all of the other default properties of a Python `LogRecord`, including `['args', 'created', 'exc_info', 'exc_text', 'filename', funcName', 'levelname', 'levelno', 'lineno', message', 'module', 'msecs', 'msg', 'name', 'pathname', 'process', 'processName', 'relativeCreated', 'stack_info']` etc.

If the `_format_by_dict` is used, only the default formatter keys are extracted from the logrecord, and then the message is added, but any additional flags are ignored.

At the moment I'm working on adding support for extracting the `extra` keys from the log record, but unfortunately the Python `Logger` implementation adds the keys to the `LogRecord.__dict__` so the only way to know what extra keys were added is to remove the default keys from that set. I will make a PR but the correct way to solve this problem may in fact be to subclass the `Logger` implementation and extract the `extra` dict at that point.

Hmm, this is an interesting discussion.  We haven't really dealt with resets before with anomaly detection, as it is really designed for streaming.  Here's what I think should happen if there are resets:

- The anomaly score right after the reset should be ignored. Nothing is predicted after a reset, so anomaly score will always be high.
- The running average buffer should be cleared out.  That's a short term estimate of anomalies and is no longer valid.
- The anomaly likelihood Gaussian statistics should be maintained and updated (except for the first one after reset as above). This is a long term estimate of the underlying system, which presumably hasn't changed.

These would be my first guesses.  I think ideally all the logic (including likelihood calculations) should be contained within an Anomaly region.

I'm not sure there is a Right Thing with io.StringIO.

Here's the situation:
subunit needs to write bytes (actual binary, not utf8 encoded text).
io.StringIO() has no bytes backing buffer, so you have to take the bytes, do something to them to make them unicode and then you can write them to the StringIO object.

We could decode with one of the codecs that can't fail like cp926 or whatever the magic # is. That seems pretty awful to put in the production code though, because its never going to be the right thing when outputting a stream - we care about the exact bytes hitting the network socket / file / inter-process pipe, and in that situation the unicode that we write to the stream would be getting re-encoded by something, potentially utf8 or whatever, and things would be entirely broken in that situation.

What are you doctesting with subunit? ... goes and looks in detail - ok - https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.testrunner/blob/master/src/zope/testrunner/tests/testrunner-subunit.txt#L44

Thats taking a subunit v1 stream, a *subset* of which are US-ASCII (but not all of) and using that as a way to check the output. But its not representative of the actual way subunit streams behave in the wild - they are line ending sensitive, for instance, so a text stream with different EOL markers will break parsing the stream. OTOH for the specific inputs you're giving it its probably pragmatically ok.

Perhaps monkey patch SpoofOut in the interim (to make https://github.com/testing-cabal/subunit/blob/master/python/subunit/__init__.py#L1296 pass - give it a read(0) method which returns b'').

Then we can talk about the best way to test your subunit support (and also support subunit-v2 which is  much more capable - native multiplexing and concurrency for instance) without the time pressure of a failing test suite.

-Rob

We're also facing a similar issue on Tastypie 0.12.1 with Django 1.7.9. I did some analysis on this (below).

I believe that rolling back to raven==5.26.0 will resolve the problem as a short term fix, but a long term fix will be needed in `raven/contrib/django/resolvers.py`. I wasn't able to find any documentation on how to running the raven-python Django tests to contribute myself, but looking at [tastypie docs on API](http://django-tastypie.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html) shows how to setup a API route which can be used for building a failing test. 

When inspecting the output of a resolver generated in a Django project using tasty pie…

```
try:
    from django.urls import get_resolver
except ImportError:
    from django.core.urlresolvers import get_resolver

resolver = get_resolver(None)
resolver.reverse_dict
```

…we see that in patterns generated by tastypie, URL Parameters are used to capture api_name (such v1) as and resource_name (such as locations):

```
u'v1/(?P<api_name>api)/(?P<resource_name>locations)/(?P<pk>.*?)/$’
```

There was a PR merged in Sept 2016 to the raven-python repo to Replace culprits with transaction names [#768](
https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/pull/768). If we check the release tags, this was merged in 5.27.0.

```
$ cd raven-python
$ git tag --contains 3e8d8f82
5.27.0
5.27.1
5.28.0
5.29.0
5.30.0
5.31.0
5.32.0
```

In these changes, a new Django resolver was introduced which replaces captured parameter values with the parameter name. With tastypie generated patterns instead of using `/v1/locations/123/`, we will see `/{api_name}/{resource_name}/{pk}/` after calls to `RouteResolver._simplify` [replaces parameter values](https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/contrib/django/resolver.py#L38) with their names.

Additionally, there is another problem where recursive calls to the raven-python `RouteResolver._resolve` function will cause `/{api_name}/` to be added to the resolved URL multiple times. This is likely happening when [generating the prefix](https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/contrib/django/resolver.py#L79) due to parents being recursively added multiple times.

@davissp14 

I think we are talking here about two different issues:
* Initial discovery of etcd cluster nodes
* Access to etcd cluster

It's possible to do initial discovery in multiple ways:
1. Dns discovery, by doing resolve of SRV or A record. We assume that these records are more or less up to date and contain at least on healthy node of etcd cluster.
2. Static discovery by doing an API call to one of nodes of Etcd cluster.
3. Static discovery via multiple nodes. It similar to 2, but if the first node in the list is not accessible, try another one. (not implemented!)

Discovery is only one side of a coin, and actually this step is not always needed. It all depends on the way how we access etcd cluster.

As one could guess, there are more than one way to access etcd cluster.
1. You access every node of etcd cluster directly. In my opinion this is a most widely used and most reliable method of accessing etcd cluster.
2. You access etcd cluster via proxy or load-balancer. Right now it is possible to specify a `etcd.proxy` in a config file and Patroni will not try to discover etcd cluster topology, but always use a given endpoint to connect.
3. Via set of proxies or load-balancers. It is not implemented directly, but still possible, if `etcd.proxy` contains not an IP, but dns name resolved into multiple IP's.

As told before, it's not hard to implement missing parts of discovery and access (3 and 3).
But there is one more missing bit. What should we do if after a long time all endpoints of etcd cluster will become not accessible? If we know that we are connecting via proxy or load-balancer, we should just continue trying. But if we are connecting to all etcd nodes directly, it is getting though, because we need to "re-run" initial discovery:
1. In case if we are relying on A or SRV - resolve them. At Zalando we are using this approach and it is proven to be reliable.
2. With static discovery the only we can do, is to try INITIAL list of etcd nodes provided in the config. Unfortunately there is no guaranty that there is some process (or somebody) keeping all Patroni config files up to date and with really high chance etcd nodes from the config don't exists anymore and therefore Patroni will never reconnect to etcd...

* Looking at your dependencies though, the only issue I see is `Chalice` itself, which should not be in the dependencies.  The `requirements.txt` is for runtime dependencies only. Chalice should be added to a `requirements-dev.txt` since it is only a development requirement not a runtime requirement. I've changed my mind in these days, now I think what we need to implement should not be a reverse proxy, a transparent proxy is enough, like a pipe and have different behavior with different destination server.

But I have new question: After this function been implemented, a probe is hard to detect whether the server is a shadowsocks server or some kind of normal server. But will it increase the characteristic of shadowsocks server while all connection is controlled by IDS?

Example: 
We have a server running shadowsocks on port 80, and transparent proxy illegal request to a legal web server which can return a 404 page.
When a probe comes, it will easily determine that this is a legal web server.
But when an IDS wiretapped some weird TCP connections , yes it's shadowsocks connections, on 80 port, it can try two things.
First is do a reply attack, to confirm this server can respond the particular data packet (although encrypted but different form 404 page), then change some bits in request, send it again, to confirm this server is trying to camouflage as a normal server(all changed request returns 404 page).
Will this behavior becomes an obvious characteristic of shadowsocks? I need your help @clowwindy

@Explorare 那个论文也就是提出了一个观点，混淆模块的作者们改进改进，这些外部特征就能和HTTPS非常相近。 混淆伪装别的协议的， 也可以根据这个论文的方法，从这几个特征点对比真实流量和混淆流量，从而改进。 
现有的GFW性能貌似不能用神经网络分析. 
他12年的论文提到，“基于机器学习的流量分类方法耗费资源较多，需要事先了解数据样本集，应用于在线环境有一定困难。”  
只要不断的更新混淆方法，减少流统计特征，这个机器学习根本学习不起来。（因为如果改进了现有的流统计特征，就得开辟新的特征用于机器学习训练，越多的特征值 ＝ 越烂的性能 ＝ 无法部署GFW）

这两篇论文，叫兽都假设了一个被动的情况，就是不会对数据流量做任何修改／干扰，也不会主动探测。
所以我猜测，如果对匿名通信做了干扰／阻断， 服务端／客户端的反应也可以作为一个特征提取点，若是作为开发者就需要更细心地混淆／伪装了。

We were able to solve the issue.
Apparently adding the proxy_config in the yaml file isn't sufficient to actually configure the proxy. 
The proxy should also be configured and passed in the oauth_client, in addition to the AdWordsClient.
(In this case, we're just passing all creds as arguments and not using the yaml file).
Please see example below for how we implemented this:

`    prxx = common.ProxyConfig.Proxy('proxy_host', proxy_port)
    proxy_config = common.ProxyConfig(http_proxy=prxx,https_proxy=prxx,disable_certificate_validation=True)

    oauth_client = oauth2.GoogleRefreshTokenClient(client_id='<INSERT_CLIENT_ID_HERE>', client_secret='<INSERT_CLIENT_SERCET_HERE>',refresh_token='<INSERT_REFRESH_TOKEN_HERE>', proxy_config=proxy_config )

    adwords_client = adwords.AdWordsClient("<INSERT_DEVELOPER_TOKEN_HERE>",oauth_client, user_agent='<INSERT_USER_AGENT_HERE>', client_customer_id='<INSERT_CLIENT_CUSTOMER_ID_HERE>', proxy_config=proxy_config)`

Hope the next person that comes across the issue finds this helpful :)
Thanks,

So, I'm still thinking you're looking for an options() method to somehow put on the SASsession; to set invocation only options. Is that right?
You can specify any of the configuration definition keys as an option on the SASsession() already. Like this:
```
sas = saspy.SASsession(cfgname='tdi', options=["-fullstimer", "-nofmterr",  "-fmtsearch" , "/pat/to/formats"])
```
You do have to set the config_option 'lock_down' : False  if you want to allowed these config values on the SASsession() to override the config def values at run-time. That is for values you have in your config definition. If you don't specify a given key in your config def, then It can already be specified on the SASsession(), even with 'lock_down' : True since that's not 'Overriding; the config def, as it's not specified.
```
SAS_config_options = {'lock_down': False,
                      'verbose'  : True
                     }
```
It sounds like you want users to be able to override these at run-time. They can then do this, or you can not put these in the config def and just specify them yourself in the SASsession() method already.

No, I don't think we have a _%saspy_ magic, to do what you're asking from the sas_kernel. The %SAS magic is to just type in SAS code, from a saspy (python kernel) session (it's nothing more than a convenience wrapper over the submit() method). Don't believe we ever made the reverse to submit saspy code from the SAS kernel session.

I found a solution that works well for me. The `%load_ext` invocation doesn't allow arguments, but cell magics do, on the same line; so I modified the `%%SAS` magic to check for an object name following its invocation, and if it is a SASsession instance, use that session to submit the code.

Check the commit here: https://github.com/jasonphillips/saspy/commit/225df5eccb102fd32bd6cbbb32ad8e895709c6a7

Usage (in place of the invocation I wrote above):

```python
%%SAS sas

proc print data=WORK.mydata; run;
```

Where, in this case, `sas` is the existing session instantiated somewhere above in the notebook by calling `saspy.SASsession()` manually. This approach actually lets you potentially manage more than one session with different workspaces and decide which one a cell will use.

The only downside I currently see is that the initial `%load_ext` call still instantiates a new session that I never end up using. If this approach makes sense to more than myself, I could start up a PR, which would require also updating the other cell magic and the documentation.

sorry the code snippet is so convoluted, i had to do some guesswork in ipython. i think you might benefit from taking a look at the following places of the library for more info:

building the datareader from the config in the archive: https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/allennlp/predictors/predictor.py#L119

the predictor uses `dataset.text_to_instance` under the hood : https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/allennlp/predictors/bidaf.py#L40 . you just need to add the label.

the model class has a `forward_on_instance` / `forward_on_instances` function (https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/allennlp/models/model.py#L116-L175), but unfortunately it uses `with torch.no_grad`. if you add an extra optional param to those functions that can toggle that `no_grad` block, you can just do `forward_on_instances([list of instances here])` and get back a list of outputs, i think.

HTH --- this might not be the optimal way to do things but it seems reasonably ok to me.

Part of the problem is that we've overloaded `--force` to have way too mean meanings at this point.  The Solver code in conda 4.4 does try to disentangle these somewhat.  We've used `--force` for at least the following:

* force-remove a package, bypassing the solver entirely
* force-install just a single package, bypassing the solver entirely,
* force-reinstall a package (i.e. uninstall then install again)
* force-remove a package that is a dependency of conda itself in the root environment
* force-clobbering paths that already exist in a new environment

The guess was quite good: COBDUMP doesn't call itself but it's header says:

```cobol
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.   COBDUMP.
      *****************************************************************
      ** This is an OpenCOBOL subroutine that will generate a        **
      ** formatted Hex/Char dump of a storage area.  To use this     **
      ** subroutine, simply CALL it as follows:                      **
      **                                                             **
      ** CALL "COBDUMP" USING <data-item>                            **
      **                    [ <length> ]                             **
      **                                                             **
```

if I change it to

```cobol
      ** C A L  L "COBDUMP" USING <data-item>                            **
```

and even if I add

```cobol
       78  COBD                 value "COBDUMP".
               CALL COBD END-CALL
```

it isn't compiled multiple times. Therefore I'd say we have multiple issues here:

1st: whatever parses this for recognizing external CALLs - it must exclude comment lines.
2nd: if possible it should parse level 78 (and 01/77 AS CONSTANT) to include them if they are referenced via CALL.
3rd (the obvious one): OCIDE should remember which modules it compiled already

And one thing I like to request about it:
Make the feature (which is indeed nice) optional [for new IDE project specifc]. I just thought about what this would done if the main program of a big software is compiled (and all 3000+ COBOL modules are generated).

Looking at the command you ran one more time, I kind of agree that one may find uploading file with an absolute path as such: "/some/file"... You could expect CLI to upload it to domain.com/mycontainer/some/file rather than to domain.com/mycontainer//some/file. 

Re. Linux - it is still not a supported file name. '/' is a reserved character for directory separation; cannot be part of the file name. However in an object store, object name can contain '/'.. So by dropping the leading '/' in CLI, we may actually break the object storage behavior just to be compatible with file system behavior.

If they have a memo key that is different from the active key, then you need to add both, the memo and the active key to your `BitShares()` instance.
Note that you can use `BitShares(keys=[wif1, wif2, wi3...])` and don't need to provide pubkey (the lib should know what to do and when to use which one).
Also note that the library can technically encode anything using any key and does not check if the webwallet will be able to decode it.

A summary of the users who are likely to face the token auth, where the default open-with-token isn't quite magical enough:

- 'notebook' browser isn't default browser, or other browser hopping (e.g. switching around for testing)
- run with `--no-browser`, or browser launching doesn't work / isn't available (e.g. remote notebooks, docker, etc.)
- use private browsing, or other pattern where cookies are cleared often

In each of these cases, the token should only be needed for the first time a browser connects to a notebook server on a given ip/port, as a cookie is used thereafter, and cookies remain valid across notebook restarts. It should be considered an 'initial setup' step, rather than an ongoing interaction with the notebook server every time you launch. Resetting cookies will require logging in again. While *browsers* share cookies across ports, we specifically don't use the same cookie for each host:port combination, because different notebook configurations could cause conflicts on the cookie secret. We could consider reversing that, as I think the experience for *most* cases would be better (i.e. all localhost notebooks are actually local). Thanks for the effort.   I think dropping Windows would be fine as the other lz4 package is linux/Mac only in Anaconda too, but I get that these things take time to figure out and debug. 

As far as installation and docker etc.  The goal was to simply be able to run:
conda install -c conda-forge py-lz4framed  (on Mac and Linux only)

As I said.  We do have a workaround by simply installing all needed gcc components into the docker image before we install py-lz4framed, but it adds 150MB to the image and takes a lot more time in the build.   
Our use case is that we are creating microservices that need to be interoperable and py-lz4framed is needed to communicate with javascript.   A lot of people doing microservices these days are moving to Docker containers and passing compressed lz4 data has benefits.   I just think that making it easier to get py-lz4framed installed will increase its use and adoption, which I think is a good thing.   I completely understand time constraints to do this though and thanks for the very quick effort!

xdist overwrites the default terminal writer because the tests order is not predictable. This means that the output:

```
coalib/tests/MakeTempTest.py ....
coalib/tests/OtherTest.py ....
```

Can't be implemented without some more complicated terminal code, because tests for `coalib/tests/OtherTest.py` might start to run before all tests from `coalib/tests/MakeTempTest.py` have finished.

I think pytest-sugar is expecting support for xdist soon in Frozenball/pytest-sugar#65, might be an alternative.

ftp://ftp.unicode.org/Public/8.0.0/ucd/EastAsianWidth.txt

    2313..231F;N     # So    [13] SEGMENT..BOTTOM RIGHT CORNER

ftp://ftp.unicode.org/Public/9.0.0/ucd/EastAsianWidth.txt

    2313..2319;N     # So     [7] SEGMENT..TURNED NOT SIGN
    231A..231B;W     # So     [2] WATCH..HOURGLASS
    231C..231F;N     # So     [4] TOP LEFT CORNER..BOTTOM RIGHT CORNER

The watch and hourglass codepoints (and maybe a whole lot more) became wide by Unicode 9.0.0.

gnome-terminal uses the new width if it's running with glib 2.50.1 or newer, whereas xterm and tmux rely on glibc whose latest release (2.24) still uses the old width.

My intention to move to Pandas TestCases is not to have "nicer code" or that we use classes without a need to.
The reason is, that these test cases really test a series or a DataFrame: they test for expected values and for the expected index.
This is especially useful because often time series are used as input for pvlib functions. My experience from everyday work with functions (not classes;-) that use Pandas and numpy is, that the preservation of Datetime indexes is sometimes a difficulty and can lead to unexpected results, that are not easy to detect.

Also for unexperienced programmers it may be usefull to have something like a template for testing, which could be provided by an existing test. 

No Problem with skipping this for now, but I think these Tests are really useful for future releases.

**First error**: Yes. This is a docker limitation. 

**Second error**: You are running it correctly, and yes this is a problem with the current pypi-version of sacred. And since `Acolyte` installs the sacred dependency with pip this fails. This problem is, in fact, the very reason I added the `replace_requirements` config entry. You can fix by adding 
`with replace_requirements.sacred="git+https://github.com/IDSIA/Sacred.git@acolyte"`.

Regarding your thoughts:
1. Docker was just my personal first choice. The idea was to have as much control over the environment as possible such that this could also be used to test reproducibility. But other options are using making a new virtualenv, or running in the current environment and only check if dependencies are met. I am open to adding these in the future. 
2. The docker images are a problem I agree. The reason I am not immediately deleting them is that when running many experiments it saves tremendous amounts of time to use the docker cache. But maybe the acolyte could clean them up when exiting. 
3. I do share your wish for a sacred command line tool, but I consider it to be on top of `Acolyte`, which was just meant as the worker thread. Ideally, we would have a command line tool, that can query the database, show status, recent runs, start workers, clean up, and many more things. Maybe the `smt` command from sumatra might be a good inspiration. So also here: yes let's do that at some point in the future ;-)

Not right now. We are in need of a design that allows us to integrate type information with binary analysis, but then we realize that the type information is useless if we don't care about type inference, data dependency, etc., which then boils down to having a data-flow analysis framework.

I'm lying down the foundation of a data-flow analysis framework right now, and as a first step towards using the type information in general, we would like to introduce the notion of "variables" into our binary analysis with an analysis routine called `VariableRecovery`. Then we will sit down and see how to better the design of type systems in angr.

As for now, it's rather a design issue than an implementation issue. While I'm happy to see PRs on it, I'd rather to have a discussion somewhere, no matter it is on GitHub, on Slack, or in person, to have a decent design first.

I have two arguments for updating ALE (at least once, but presumably every now and again):

- Fixes, such as highlighted for BreakOut. These may alter semantics, and so they could require a version difference and would have to be incorporated with care.
- New functionality, like loading/store system state (include pseudorandomness) that is useful for experiments that need to restore past states. This in particular makes no difference in the environment semantics.  I'm raising this issue b.c. I have experiments that I'd like to port from direct ALE to the gym that require the `decodeState(), restoreSystemState()` methods.

For mechanism, I think a submodule is reasonable. This is what I've been doing in my own projects and it hasn't been any trouble. I'd be happy to help with this switch-over. Furthermore, the submodule preserves the versioning and authorship information from upstream, and so gives full attribution to the work.

As far as I can tell from the [GraphQL spec](https://facebook.github.io/graphql/#sec-Response-Format) there is no discussion of http response codes at all. The status codes of HTTP are strongly tied to rest principles. It's not clear to me whether GraphQL APIs should distinguish between 2XX and 4XX status codes.

Most notably, you can batch multiple mutations into the same request to the server. If one successfully creates a resource (201), one mutates a resource (200), one fails validation (400) and another the target object does not exists (404), what should the return value from the API be?

At the moment, GraphQL APIs seem to err on the side of 200 unless the server blows up, in which case 500.

About version compatibility, I think it depends, and should be addressed case by case:
- Some settings are not so critical, for example, children table. It is not so dangerous to delete children table due to the ChildInfo version change, Thread network could recover by itself. And of cause, no re-commissioning required after firmware update with this kind of settings version change.
- Some settings are critical, for example, active dataset. It is dangerous to delete active dataset, which may cause the device could not reattach the previous network and require re-commissioning. With `/home/user/src/cmd2` in PYTHONPATH, it not only puts `cmd2` in your import path, but every egg installed in place in `..src/cmd2` is on your import path too. I suggest the following:

1. Fix your shell init (if using bash it's in `.profile` or `.bash_profile` or `.bashrc`) to not set PYTHONPATH. Log out and log in again and ensure no PYTHONPATH environment variable exists.

2. Install `cmd2` "in-place" by:
```
$ cd /home/user/src/cmd2
$ sudo pip install -e .
```
This method still makes any changes to the `cmd2` source code instantly available, and has the added benefit of making the package show up when you do `pip list`.

3. Remove eggs and cached python byte code:
```
$ cd /home/user/src/cmd2
$ rm -rf .eggs
$ rm -rf *.egg-info
$ rm -rf *.egg
$ find . -name __pycache__ -exec rm -rf {} \;
```
That last command finds and removes all the byte code caches.

4. Re-run the import profiler (again):
```
$ python -c 'import cProfile
> cProfile.run("import cmd2")
' > anselor-import3.txt
```
and attach `anselor-import3.txt` to this thread.

Porting from optparse to argparse looks like it will be complicated by two aspects of how their APIs are incompatible:

1. argparse has no equivalent of the optparse.make_option() factory function which returns an Option instance.

2. For optparse, the parse_args() method returns a tuple of (options, args):
     (options, args) = parser.parse_args()
   But for argparse, the parse_args() method just returns an args object:
    args = parser.parse_args() which includes both options and positional arguments.
   This requires you to add additional ArgumentParser.add_argument() calls for the positional arguments.

Hi Sergey,

this is a know limitation of pybind11. The trouble is that ``typeid(*ptr)`` (which pybind11 uses internally to determine the type of the return value) returns the typeid of ``C``, which is not known to pybind11. The fact that ``B`` is a parent of ``C`` is also not known at runtime because there is no way of traversing ``typeid`` hierarchies. So it's basically a limitation of C++ itself that manifests itself in pybind11.

Best,
WEnzel

The gist is that `enum_` supported defining methods like `class_` but this was undocumented and would also break chaining, i.e. `enum_().value().def().value()` would not compile. The first `class_` API call would implicitly turn the `enum_` into a `class_`. This PR makes that explicit, documented and tested, but this will break the old undocumented behavior.

Thanks for the suggestion.

I don't think this is a good default behavior, because the exact difficulties that you mention are likely to be common for a number of users. Other CAs permit overlapping validity of multiple certificates because we don't know for sure the schedule on which users will roll out the new certs, particularly in the case where the certs are served by multiple devices, such as multiple different web servers. Revoking by default upon renewal will force people to deploy the new cert almost instantaneously. In our original renewal design, we contemplated even waiting one week (!!) before trying to roll it out in order to give the user a chance to manually verify that the renewed cert was correct. Although we didn't end up retaining that one-week delay idea, it still seems plausible to me that some users would prefer such a delay because of their particular setups.

While it is definitely good practice to revoke certs that are definitely going to be unused, I just don't think we can make that assumption on the user's behalf in general, because we would end up surprising people and breaking their sites. If you can think of a way to make it optional, I would support having the option available in Certbot somehow for people who know they want it (maybe as a flag --revoke-when-renewing or something).

Adding here notes from our discussions on Raiden and client RPC connections during the Fuerte Ventura Retreat:

## Communication Protocol Used by Our RPC connection

JSON-RPC via an instance of pyethapp.rpc_client which is essentially
https://tinyrpc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/. It's making JSONRPC calls via HTTP


## Functions currently used when interfacing with an ethereum client

eth.blockNumber
eth_uninstallFilter
deploy_solidity_contract
eth_get_filterChanges
call()
polling transaction calls

### Problems

- It seems we are creating too many RPC connections and we must find a way around it:
https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/253

- We should be doing caching. What kind of caching strategy should we be doing?

##Ideas

- We have too many calls to the ethereum node, so we should try to reduce the number of node calls. Improve/implement caching?
- Poll only if there is a new block mined, to save RPC calls
- Poll for any event in a particular contract address and filter them on the application side depending on the results
- Why are we creating too many RPC connections when making client calls? Related to: https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/253
- What should be cached? Block number and all properties of the channel we should try to cache and not have to make calls to the client for every single time we want to query a property of the channel.
- Question: How do we handle chain reorganizations? What kind of caching strategy should we use in order to tackle chain
reorganizations? i.e.: If your cache has been updated with the value of a losing chain, then a chain reorganization happens and you need to update your cache. How do we handle that?

Yeah, interesting, custom objects seem to be a bit slower than primitive types (presumably since it has to go back to Python, whereas for a list of ints it never has to leave C++).

E.g., it's slightly faster if you do

```python
x_id = ray.put(1)

%time ray.put(10000000 * [x_id.id()])  # 1.5s

%time ray.put(10000000 * [x_id])  # 26s
```

You can reproduce the ID out of the byte string since `x_id == ray.local_scheduler.ObjectID(x_id.id())`, although this is a hack and not part of the API.

But yes, this will all be handled much more nicely once we do it properly.

While there is a fundamental design tension underlying this specific decision, I think it's best to minimize the amount of functional overlap in ONNX operators. While it is convenient for frontends to have many op choices to map to when exporting (FC, GEMM, Dot), I think the extra amount of effort needed for importers leads to a net loss.

In addition, by pushing on importers and exporters to interop with a smaller set of operators, it encourages stronger pattern matching tools/functionality which is ultimately more flexible and future proof.

For example, introducing GEMM in #47 does not add any expressive power to ONNX since the same functionality can already be represented by Add, Dot, and Transpose with appropriate pattern matching in importers _that need it_, while adding it as an op requires that _all_ importers and exporters have to reason about it.
